With higher financing rates and oversupply lowering its occupancy rate from over 96% to below 94%, shares of BRT fell 45% from their 2022 high, bottoming at $14.17 in August 2025, representing a 54-month low. They haven’t rebounded much at all, currently off 45% from that peak.

That said, in some instances, the market dynamics are starting to take a turn to the positive. In response to the recent saturation, 2025 U.S. apartment starts are projected to be down nearly 50% from their 2022 top of 531,000, with declines in the Sun Belt region expected to be meaningfully above the national average. Furthermore, the rent-versus-buy proposition in America is heavily weighted towards renting, with markets in the Sun Belt, such as Charlotte, Houston, Dallas/Ft. Worth, Raleigh/Durham, Atlanta, and Nashville all representing 50% to 60% discounts to rent. So with the continuing headwind (for now) of record supply meeting the tailwind of extreme cost to own, BRT is trading at just under 10 times TTM Adj. AFFO of $1.48 per share.

Against those general market dynamics, BRT has 21% of its mortgages outstanding (totaling $108.9 million) at a weighted average interest rate of 4.27% that will rollover between July 1, 2025, and December 31, 2026. Its July 2025 80% acquisition of a 214-unit multi-family facility in Auburn, Alabama for $36.5 million (including the assumption of an interest-only $24 million mortgage) provides some insight into its future refinancing outlook, with the interest rate on the fixed rate loan that matures in 2032 at 5.38%, somewhat in line with the 5.22% interest-only rate it received as part of a $27.4 million mortgage financing executed for a property in Georgia during 2024. That mortgage matures in 2031. With a weighted average interest rate of 4.26% on its entire portfolio and a weighted average term to maturity of 3.4 years (as of June 30, 2025), this undercurrent of higher refinancing rates will continue to erode BRT’s future FFO and AFFO calculations by $0.03 to $0.06 per annum, ceteris paribus.

Also creating a bit of a headwind is the fact that the REIT’s management team has not engaged the Street since early 2024 and does a subpar job of keeping the investor relations section of its website current, making it challenging to obtain insight into its current thinking. BRT is a family affair, with former Chairman of the Board and current director Fredric Gould’s two sons Jeff and Matt in the positions of CEO and senior vice president, respectively. Their cousin, Mitchell, is an executive vice president. Through their Gould Investors L.P., personal stakes, and other investment entities, they own a ~27% stake in the REIT.

Q2 '25 Financials:

BRT posted Q2 '25 FFO of $0.29 per share and AFFO of $0.36 per share on revenue of $24.2 million versus FFO of $0.29 per share and AFFO of $0.35 per share on revenue of $23.9 million in Q2 '24. As for the quarter, there was very little to report, with both property adds occurring after the close of the quarter.

Q3 '25 Financials:

BRT Apartments posted its Q3 results on November 8th. FFO fell from the second quarter to just 28 cents a share of FFO. Revenues were flat on a year-over-year basis to $24.43 million. There were very few other data points provided with the company's third quarter press release.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

As for BRT’s balance sheet, the carrying value on its apartment investments of $602.1 million and cash of $23.6 million were offset by mortgages and related obligations of just under $500 million, as of the close of Q3. After the announcement of its latest purchase, an 80% ownership interest in a 150-unit complex in Savannah, Georgia, BRT had $15 million outstanding on a revolving credit facility, which it plans to pay off through a refinancing in December 2025.

BRT’s two quarterly disbursements of $0.25 per share in 1H25 were easily covered by its AFFO of $0.75 per share, representing a current yield of 6.9% and a solid payout ratio of 67%.

BRT has a small Wall Street following. Colliers Securities reiterated its Hold rating and $16 price target on the stock following Q3 results hitting the wires. B Riley Financial reiterated its Buy rating on the stock in late September but lowered its price target from $21.00 a share to $19.50. In early April, Citizens JMP reissued its Buy rating and $24 a share price target on BRT. On average, sell-side analysts expect BRT to generate FFO of $1.17 a share on revenue of $95.8 million in FY25, followed by FFO of $1.21 a share on revenue of $99.7 million in FY26.

The Gould clan is perpetually bullish on its stock, through additions to Gould Investors, L.P. and personal accounts. This entity has purchased approximately $1.5 million in shares so far in the fourth quarter.

Verdict:

For an investor looking for yield, BRT is a decent play, returning 6.7% with a currently solid 67% payout ratio. That said, AFFO per share will erode going forward as it is compelled to gradually refinance its low-interest mortgages, inflating its payout ratio. For those betting on Fed rate reductions coming to the rescue, there is no guarantee that the reduction of an overnight rate will drive rates lower at the back-end of the yield curve. In fact, even with the 10-year treasury’s (US10Y) recent move nearer 4%, its yield is roughly flat since the Fed began its easing cycle in the summer of 2024, a period that has seen the Fed Funds rate decline by 125 basis points to 4.25%.

That said, although its yield is solid and its forward P/E on FY26E FFO of 12 is investible, the price appreciation outlook for its shares seems muted given the aforementioned headwinds – namely, oversupply leading to pressure on rents and occupancy, as well as higher refinancing rates. With very low volume in its stock, option activity is nearly non-existent, making a covered call basically non-viable. As such, due to muted price appreciation potential, no investment is recommended.
